entities.name: Pharaoh's magicians
entities.description: Magicians in the service of Pharaoh, whose works were compared to Moses' miracles and later critiqued as illusions or 'juggling knacks'.

entities.name: St. Augustine
entities.description: Cited as a hold of that they made by art the serpents, etc.

entities.name: J. Bap. Neap
entities.description: Author cited for information on the production of monsters, serpents, and the nature of toads/frogs.

entities.name: Plutarch
entities.description: Cited regarding the descent of toads in rain.

entities.name: Aelianus
entities.description: Cited regarding seeing frogs and toads with earth-like parts.

entities.name: Dati7natiis Hispanus
entities.description: Claimed to be able to make creatures at will.

entities.name: Jamblichus
entities.description: Cited regarding the nature of fascination and imagination.

entities.name: Calvine
entities.description: Cited as describing Pharaoh's magicians as jugglers.

entities.name: Erasmus
entities.description: Cited regarding the argument that witchcraft is old wives' fables.

entities.name: Clemens
entities.description: Cited regarding the magicians' works being prestigious shows rather than true miracles.

entities.name: the Cockatrice
entities.description: A venomous serpent produced by melting arsenic and serpent poison in an egg.

entities.name: Perspective Glasses
entities.description: Optical devices used to create illusions, such as making things appear far or near, or upside down.

entities.name: Witchcraft/Conjuring
entities.description: The practice of performing supernatural feats through words, charms, and illusions.

entities.name: Magic/Art Magike
entities.description: The use of 'art' or 'cunning' to produce monsters and illusions.

entities.name: the Devil
entities.description: The entity that some claim cannot create creatures smaller than a barleycorn.

summary: This passage from Reginald Scot's 'Discoverie of Witchcraft' explores the distinction between true miracles performed by God through prophets like Moses, and the miracles attributed to witches or magicians, which are characterized as illusions, 'juggling knacks,' or optical deceptions. The text discusses various methods for producing monsters and monsters via 'art magike' and's' using perspective glasses and sleight of hand (juggling).
